animation.round_start: Round {{round}} starts: Assume Index {{i}} is the minimum
animation.compare_true: Compare: Is Index {{j}} ({{scanVal}}) less than current minimum ({{minVal}})? -> True
animation.compare_false: Compare: Is Index {{j}} ({{scanVal}}) less than current minimum ({{minVal}})? -> False
animation.update_min: Found a smaller value! Update minimum index to {{minIdx}}
animation.swap_true: Swap: Exchange minimum of this round (Index {{minIdx}}) with Index {{i}}
animation.swap_false: Index {{i}} is already the minimum, no swap needed

relatedProblems.215: Selection sort concept: find the K-th largest element using partial sorting
relatedProblems.414: Finding max/min: find the third maximum number in an array
relatedProblems.164: Advanced sorting: find the maximum gap between adjacent elements after sorting

sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: Selection Sort is an intuitive **comparison sort**. Its [[primary|core essence]] is to find the **minimum value** from the unsorted region each pass and swap it with the **first element** of that region, repeating until fully sorted.
items: The array is divided into a **sorted** and an **unsorted** region; each iteration gradually expands the [[primary|sorted range]]
Each pass scans the remaining **unsorted region**, continuously comparing and recording the exact position of the [[primary|minimum value]]
Once the minimum is found, an **element swap** places it at the [[primary|first position]] of the unsorted region to lock it in

sections.heading: Operational Complexity
sections.content: Regardless of the initial data order, Selection Sort must scan the unsorted region to find the minimum, so its time complexity is fixed at [[danger|O(n²)]] for best, average, and worst cases alike.
items: Comparison operations: a nested loop compares elements one by one to find the minimum, fixed at [[danger|O(n²)]]
Swap operations: at most one element swap is performed per pass after the minimum is found, giving [[warning|O(n)]]
Memory space: an in-place sort requiring only constant auxiliary variables, with space complexity [[success|O(1)]]

sections.heading: What Can Be Optimized
sections.content: Selection Sort's strengths are **in-place sorting** and very few **swap operations**, making it suitable for small-scale or write-expensive environments. It performs poorly on large datasets and should be replaced there.
items: **Memory-constrained environments**: no extra space allocation needed — highly advantageous in resource-tight **embedded systems**
**High write-cost scenarios**: at most one swap per pass significantly reduces **data exchange count** and lowers hardware burden
**Not suitable for large datasets**: extremely inefficient on large data — switch to **Merge Sort** or **Quick Sort** instead
**Not suitable for nearly-sorted data**: lacks an early-exit mechanism — **Insertion Sort** is recommended for such cases

sections.heading: Problems It Fits Best
sections.content: Selection Sort shines when available memory is minimal or when data swaps and writes are extremely costly. Look for these clues to recognize it:
items: **Resource-constrained array sorting**: problem limits extra space, requiring in-place sorting with O(1) space complexity
**Minimizing data swap count**: context emphasizes high write costs or caps total swaps — each pass swaps at most once
**Fixed performance and step count**: problem demands that execution time and comparison count are fully predictable regardless of initial order
**Finding extremes implementation**: a foundational algorithm exercise verifying mastery of nested loops and unsorted-region minimum logic

sections.heading: Sort Stability
sections.content: Selection Sort is an [[warning|unstable sort]]. When performing **element swaps**, it may disrupt the **original relative order** of equal values in the sequence — a conceptual blind spot that beginners easily overlook.
items: [[warning|Unstable sort]] characteristic: when duplicate values exist, their **original relative order** may differ after sorting
The **element swap** trap: swapping the minimum to the front can move the front element behind other equal-valued elements
A beginner's **conceptual blind spot**: newcomers focus on numeric value alone, ignoring that many real scenarios require preserving the initial order of equal elements
**Stability optimization** approach: to achieve stability, replace direct swapping with an insertion-style shift that moves elements one position at a time

sections.heading: Comparison with Other Sorts
sections.content: Beginners often confuse Selection Sort with basic **Bubble Sort** or **Insertion Sort**. A dedicated comparison of their **swap counts** and characteristics helps learners build a clearer algorithmic framework.
items: vs. **Bubble Sort**: Bubble Sort swaps adjacent elements frequently; Selection Sort swaps at most once per pass, making its [[success|write cost]] minimal
vs. **Insertion Sort**: Insertion Sort can exit early on sorted data; Selection Sort has no such mechanism, always running at [[danger|O(n²)]]
**Stability** difference: Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort are both stable; Selection Sort disrupts the relative order of equal elements, making it an [[warning|unstable sort]]
